extracts.extract: Avoid 'tool soup, which is trying to put a pinch of everything into a story, for the sake of seeming thorough or well-rounded. Often one single tool alone is enough to tell a story at a higher plane. It is easy to go overboard and overwhelm the reader.' Put the story first and foremost, then try to find the tools to tell it.
extracts.chapter: A Cautionary Tale: It's About the Journalism, Not Just the Tools

extracts.extract: Pick-Stick-Dig-Dump framework for evaluating tools: (1) Pick an app that fills a specific need after checking reviews and privacy settings, (2) Stick with it by using it in daily coverage, (3) Dig into it for two to three months asking whether it saves time or enhances work, (4) Dump it after three months if it does not prove its value.

When evaluating tools, assess four core attributes: Quality (does it help produce better or different work?), Time (does it save time or reduce friction?), Cost (can the newsroom and audience afford it?), and Reliability (is the tool likely to persist and is its team committed?).

AI will transform journalism in five areas: Organizing (natural language queries over gathered information), Converting (translating and summarizing across languages and formats), Monitoring (tracking subtle changes in government and corporate data over time), Analyzing (identifying outliers and patterns in complex datasets), and Presenting (generating charts, infographics, audio/video versions of stories).

Teach journalism concepts, not tools. Tools are a vehicle for reporting, not a means to an end. Combine old-school shoe-leather reporting with cutting-edge digital techniques to produce data-driven and multimedia stories. Sprinkle ethics, editing and journalism into the software lessons rather than preaching ethics in long standalone slideshows.
extracts.chapter: Teaching Digital Concepts, Not Just Tools

extracts.extract: Be a reporter first -- this is the skill from which all else flows. Be a reader second -- channel your own reading behaviors to inform what you produce. Tell one story at a time or make one comparison (X vs. Y axis) at a time. If you have to explain what your tool is telling the reader, you have made it more complicated than you need. Quality over quantity: you do not need endless maps, charts and graphs on a single story.

There is no magic to setting up a good reporting workflow. Research before you go, and then research some more. Ask basic questions: What am I writing and why? What do I want or need to get from this source and when and how are the best ways to do that?
extracts.chapter: Getting Organized: Establish a Digital Workflow

extracts.extract: For longer stories, keep a high-level timeline and short bios of key players for quick reference. Maintain a spreadsheet of sources with title, phone number, email, contact dates and follow-ups. Build separate folders for images, maps, data, and records so they can be accessed quickly as both reporting resources and story embeds.
extracts.chapter: Getting Organized: Establish a Digital Workflow

extracts.extract: Use a Google Drive file-naming system that makes sense: Assignment_(Name)_Date_StorySlugNOTES or Assignment_Names_(Dates)_Story slug. Check with editors for a specific naming system.

Nine rules for writing for the web: (1) Get the most newsworthy information in the first paragraph in 25-30 words, (2) Link to sources whenever you reference information, (3) Hotlink phrases not full URLs at four to five words maximum, (4) Indent quotes using blockquote, (5) Use brief paragraphs with 1 idea, 2-3 sentences, 4-5 typed lines, (6) Write literal SEO headlines under 75 characters in subject-verb-object structure, (7) Split articles with subheadings, (8) End with a call to action or what happens next, (9) Embed linked media such as tweets, videos, audio or images.
extracts.chapter: Get the News in the Lead

extracts.extract: Great reporting drives great writing. If you cannot research, pull public records, interview sources and gather information, then your story will not work. It does not matter how talented a writer you are. You cannot write what you do not know, what you have not heard, what you have not seen.

Covering a major story over several years requires next-level organization. Keep a spreadsheet of who is testifying in order, with ages, key details of testimony and background, linked to recorded testimony files. At end of each day, capture audio files with transcripts and link them to the testimony spreadsheet. Keep key prior stories in a Google Doc for quick reference.

When filing FOI requests, be specific about the type of record, what dates the records cover and what the topic is. Simply asking for 'all of the mayor's email correspondence' is too broad. Requesting the mayor's emails over the past three years discussing the public funding of a new bridge with the city's chief financial officer is more specific and gives the agency a better roadmap to find the records. This typically cuts down on denials.

Triangulating journalistic sources: data, documents and human sources work in concert with one another when reporting a story. Reporters use public records, data analysis and interviews together to strengthen stories.

It is particularly important to use a diverse group of experts in your stories regardless of topic. Many newsrooms now track diversity in their coverage -- cross-sections of the community, how they are quoted, where they appear, and that includes expert sources. Always vet expert sources: read beyond their bio, search their name for trouble, check if they are being paid by a company to shape what they tell you, and search Google Scholar for their publications.

Best advice for reporters using transcription tools: proceed at your own risk. The tools are fine for routine interviews and public meetings. But think twice before using them with sensitive stories and interview subjects because cloud-based services share data with third parties and respond to law enforcement requests, creating privacy and security vulnerabilities for confidential sources. For sensitive information, use a non-cloud recorder and transcribe manually.
extracts.chapter: Using Transcription Tools to Speed Reporting

extracts.extract: Use transcription tools only to check quotes and for context when writing. Also take handwritten notes. Taking physical notes signals to sources that you are working. In advanced courses, require students to upload the audio file, the transcript and their handwritten notes with full source name and contact info. Spot check these files and use them to fact-check work when grading.
extracts.chapter: Using Transcription Tools to Speed Reporting

extracts.extract: An old reporter's trick: do not transcribe the entire interview. As you record, keep track of the time code on the recorder. In your notebook, list the times when an interesting quote starts or ends, and work forward or backward from there. This saves many hours of time with transcriptions.

Self-editing process: step away from the story, clear your head, then read start to finish without editing as the reader would see it. Then make an editing pass for grammar, AP Style, punctuation, usage, spelling. Make a second pass for fact-checking names, data and hotlinks. Run grammar and spellcheck. Write the headline last. For longer stories, printing and editing by hand catches mistakes that backlit screens make eyes lazy to.
extracts.chapter: Editing Your Work and Writing Headlines

extracts.extract: You must fact-check any AI-generated content for plagiarism, falsehoods, outdated information and biased or made-up information. Think of AI as making suggestions: you can accept or reject them the same way you would grammar check or spell check. AI should work for you, not vice versa.
extracts.chapter: Editing Your Work and Writing Headlines

extracts.extract: Write SEO headlines in present tense and subject-verb-object structure (who-did-what) at 8-10 words or around 75 characters maximum containing keywords from the story. Do not use puns and metaphors because search engines are literal. Do not write the headline as a title or label. Use Google Trends to test which keywords are searched more to choose the better headline term.

The general rule when searching for a person, a story, or just about anything, is to start specific and work your way out from there. Type the exact term using quotes to denote an exact phrase and see what comes up. More often you will need to cut out a word or search for a more umbrella term, and then skim or read whatever comes up.

Use 'filetype:' search operator to find specific data formats (xlsx, pdf, doc, ppt) on the open web. Use 'site:' operator to search within a specific website, which is particularly helpful with government websites that have poor built-in search engines. Use the AROUND operator in all caps to find two terms within a specified number of words of each other.

Set up Google Alerts with search operators to monitor specific agencies. Example: site:lla.la.gov investigative ('new orleans' OR 'orleans parish') pings you when the Louisiana Legislative Auditor posts a new investigative audit mentioning New Orleans. Google Alerts arrive within a day of a new result -- not instantaneous but good for staying on top of specific files, agencies or news topics.

Google Dataset Search lets you search millions of datasets posted by academic researchers, nonprofits, companies and government agencies. Filter results by when data was posted, format and whether you must pay. Google does not fact-check the datasets -- it merely guides you to them and provides information so you can vet the data yourself. It is an incredibly efficient tool for finding clean datasets on deadline.

Be careful not to draw assumptions from Google Trends surges. Search the dates in Google News thoroughly before using them in a story. Explain the Trends chart y-axis to readers -- the 0 to 100 represents a normalization, not actual traffic numbers. When attributing results, use 'According to an analysis of Google search data.' Do not draw gross generalizations: just because candidates are searched more does not mean they are more popular.

Use the Wayback Machine 'Changes' tool to see when a web page was updated. Select two captures and click Compare to see side-by-side views. This is helpful for seeing if an organization is changing or hiding something on its website.

When searching an online database, you can sometimes get it to return all of the records by leaving the search bar blank. You can also try entering wildcards like '*' and '%%'. These tricks do not work with every database, but they are always worth trying.

To find older datasets on government sites that only show current data, hack the web address. Agencies have consistent URLs for naming files -- simply change the ID, date or section in a URL to pull up archived documents. Example: changing '20190430' to '20180430' in a DOD report URL retrieves the same location's report from a year earlier.
extracts.chapter: Hacking a Web Address to Find Archived Data

extracts.extract: Steps to debunk fake news: (1) Think before sharing and read beyond the headline, (2) Search if other outlets are reporting it, (3) Check fact-checking sites like Snopes and PolitiFact and Google Fact Check Explorer, (4) Check the outlet and author's credibility and social media, (5) Look at the publish date and time, (6) Examine links and sources used -- are they attributed and current?, (7) Look for questionable quotes and AI-generated image tells like disproportionate eyes/ears/hands, (8) Beware of confirmation bias.

When fact-checking images, assess five dimensions: Provenance (is it the original content? does it look altered?), Source (who captured it? use reverse image search), Date (when was it captured?), Location (where was it captured? use Google Earth), and Motivation (why was it captured? consider the context and reputation of who is sharing it).

Use reverse image search in Google Images and TinEye interchangeably as they run different algorithms and provide sometimes different results. Scroll through results to find the earliest posts and see where the photo first appeared. Use the Tools button to filter by date. If a photo may be doctored, use FotoForensics to check metadata and see if the photo has been layered and flattened/compressed.
extracts.chapter: How to Do a Reverse Image Search

extracts.extract: Solutions for building trust with audiences: Be Right (explain how stories developed and changed over time), Be Your Community (be knowledgeable about local community issues), Be Real (be authentic and own up to mistakes), Be Neutral (leave personal opinions out of news coverage). If just one journalist from a local news source is not trusted, audiences are overwhelmingly less likely to use the source overall.

Apply a scientific method to building audience campaigns: (1) Outline a specific audience goal at the beginning, (2) Formulate hypotheses to test, (3) Track as much data as you can using distinct bit.ly URLs to measure click-through rates, (4) At campaign end, revisit original hypotheses -- moments when hypotheses are wrong are often most useful, (5) Document findings immediately, including what you would do differently.
extracts.chapter: Developing Social Audiences at Large Newsrooms

extracts.extract: Steps to verify information found on social media: (1) Be skeptical -- always read past the headline, (2) Review the original source's social media accounts for reliability, (3) Cross-check with other sources and contact directly, (4) Check the geo-location of the post, (5) Seek corroboration on social media via image search and other eyewitness accounts, (6) Seek official corroboration by calling authorities, (7) If an image seems suspicious, reverse image search it, (8) Use Google Earth to verify photo locations.

Twitter threads are an effective way to roll out investigative projects. Keep a 'data diary' of the reporting, analysis and writing/editing process, then explain that reporting process to the reader through the thread. This makes your reporting transparent and builds trust with the reader. Incorporating graphics, video and photos into threads helps readers notice them in feeds and conveys data and background.

Use analytics to look at what you can change or improve in coverage, design or presentation rather than what can be cut. Track social analytics over several weeks and months before making brash decisions about adjusting coverage and resources. Export Twitter analytics as spreadsheets and sort by impressions, engagements, replies and URL clicks to find patterns in which topics, days and times perform best.

Crowdsourcing is where mainstream media ask for or search for eyewitness accounts from breaking stories they are not staffing or have not arrived at yet. It is best practice to ask the person posting who shot the photo and get that person's permission to use it, though you can embed tweets rights-free.
extracts.chapter: Social Media: Past, Present and Future

extracts.extract: Do not speculate on social media -- write only what you observe or know. Never delete tweets, but correct errors. Alert your audience when you will be live-tweeting. Include observations and try to put the reader into the scene. Live-tweeting is the best on-deadline writing: there is little time to overwrite or overthink, which produces the most powerful inverted pyramid leads.
extracts.chapter: More Social Media Tips from Chris Graves for Covering Trials and Breaking News

extracts.extract: Fact-checkers need automated tools from platforms that match fact-check articles to misinformation that is being repeated to enable them to work faster. Politicians often repeat the same false claims several times, so building a library of already-debunked claims enables near-real-time fact-checking of future speeches.
extracts.chapter: Fact-Checking and the Rise of Fake News

extracts.extract: For publications of any size, think about what the primary audience objective is with any platform and then make sure your work there supports that goal. When approaching existing online communities like Reddit, bring added value to a discussion within your coverage areas -- host an AMA with reporters who can provide expertise or comment on trending threads with additional context from previous reporting.
extracts.chapter: Developing Social Audiences at Large Newsrooms
